1: 'Cut for yourself two tablets of stone like the first, and I will write on the tablets the words that were on the first tablets, which you broke': The second tablets differ from the first in one detail: Moses cuts them, God writes on them (v28 confirms God wrote the words). The first tablets were entirely God's work (Exodus 31:18); the second require Moses's labor. The covenant renewal is genuine but not identical to the original — Israel's sin has changed the terms of the relationship. The phrase 'which you broke' (asher shibarta) is noted without condemnation; the breaking was a covenantally necessary act, not a sin.
2: 'Be ready in the morning, and come up in the morning to Mount Sinai, and present yourself there to me on the top of the mountain': The morning timing of theophany is consistent with the earlier Sinai events (Exodus 19:16, Exodus 24:4) and with the liturgical pattern of dawn as the prime time of divine encounter (Psalms 30:5, 'weeping may tarry for the night, but joy comes with the morning'). The mountain ascent without the people mirrors Moses's first ascent; the renewal is bilateral between YHWH and Moses, not a communal event.
3: 'No one shall come up with you, and let no one be seen throughout all the mountain. Let no flocks or herds graze opposite that mountain': The exclusion of all other persons and even animals replicates the conditions of the original Sinai theophany (Exodus 19:12-13, 24) but strips it down to the minimum: Moses alone. The prohibition on animals grazing near the mountain reflects the contagious holiness of the theophanic site — sacred space radiates outward and cannot be casually entered. The fence-around-the-mountain principle is intensified by the golden calf catastrophe: there are no deputies this time.
4: So Moses cut two tablets of stone like the first. And he rose early in the morning and went up on Mount Sinai, as YHWH had commanded him, and took in his hand two tablets of stone: The compliance formula confirms Moses's obedience precisely to the instructions of vv1-3: the same tablets, the same timing, the same ascent. The spare narrative — Moses carrying the tablets up the mountain — sets the scene for one of the most theologically concentrated texts in the entire Pentateuch.
5: YHWH descended in the cloud and stood with him there, and proclaimed the name of YHWH: The cloud descent — the pillar of cloud as the vehicle of theophany — is the same medium as at the tent of meeting (Exodus 33:9) and the Tabernacle (Exodus 40:34-35). The verb vayiqra beshem YHWH (and he proclaimed in the name of YHWH) can be read as either YHWH proclaiming his own name or Moses proclaiming YHWH's name; the parallelism with v6 (where the subject is clearly YHWH) and the Jewish liturgical tradition both favor YHWH self-proclaiming. The name-proclamation in vv6-7 is the substance of what was promised in Exodus 33:19 (I will proclaim my name YHWH before you).
6: YHWH passed before him and proclaimed, 'YHWH, YHWH, a God merciful (rachum) and gracious (chanun), slow to anger (erekh apayim), and abounding in steadfast love (chesed) and faithfulness (emet)': The opening four attributes of what Jewish tradition calls the Shelosh Esreh Middot (13 divine attributes, vv6-7) constitute the most important systematic statement of God's character in the Hebrew Bible. The double repetition of YHWH, YHWH has been variously interpreted: God's name proclaimed twice for emphasis; the God of the past (before the sin) and present (after the sin) who remains the same; or the two covenantal moments (the original giving and the renewal). Rachum (compassionate, from rechem, womb — the deep maternal compassion of a mother for her child) and chanun (gracious, disposition of undeserved favor) are the foundational pair. Erekh apayim (literally long of nostrils — the idiom for patient restraint of anger) uses the physical image of controlled breathing. Chesed (steadfast love/loyal covenant love) and emet (faithfulness/truth) are the paired covenant-relationship virtues.
7: 'Keeping steadfast love for thousands, forgiving iniquity and transgression and sin, but who will by no means clear the guilty, visiting the iniquity of the fathers on the children and the children's children, to the third and the fourth generation': The 13th attribute (by traditional counting) and the limit: YHWH will by no means clear (venaqqeh lo yenaqqeh, an absolute negative formed with the infinitive absolute) the guilty. The juxtaposition of unlimited forgiveness (for thousands) and unavoidable accountability (to the fourth generation) captures the essential tension in biblical theology of judgment and mercy. The paqad avon avot (visiting the iniquity of the fathers) is not inherited guilt but the social consequence of sin that spreads through communities and generations; the Decalogue version (Exodus 20:5) identifies it specifically with idol-worship. Ezekiel 18 later argues against any mechanical generational guilt, clarifying that each individual is accountable for their own sin.
8: And Moses quickly bowed his head toward the earth and worshiped: Vayimmaher Mosheh vayiqod artza vayishtachu (Moses hurried and bowed to the earth and worshiped) — the immediacy of the prostration (maher, quickly) signals the overwhelming impact of the theophany. The man who spoke to God 'face to face as a friend' (Exodus 33:11) collapses into immediate adoration at the proclamation of the divine character. The worship gesture is earth-directed: the full body pressed to the ground, the face down — the maximal acknowledgment of the chasm between divine and human.
9: And he said, 'If now I have found favor in your sight, O Lord, please let the Lord go in the midst of us, for it is a stiff-necked people, and pardon our iniquity and our sin, and take us for your inheritance': Moses's petition immediately follows the divine character proclamation — he uses the attributes just declared as the ground for his request. Sacha avoneinu vechatateinu (pardon our iniquity and our sin) — the prayer for forgiveness cites the two terms just used in v7 as forgiven by God. The request to be God's nachalah (inheritance/portion) reverses the relational movement: not Israel claiming God as their inheritance but Israel asking to be claimed as God's possession. Deuteronomy 9:26,29 uses the same nachalah vocabulary in Moses's retelling of this prayer.
10: And he said, 'Behold, I am making a covenant. Before all your people I will do marvels such as have not been created in all the earth or in any nation': The covenant renewal is explicitly called a new making (karet berit, cutting a covenant) — the same language as the original covenant of Exod 24. The promise of unprecedented marvels (niflaot) anticipates the conquest; the phrase 'never been created in all the earth or among any nation' uses creation language (nibra'u, from bara, the same verb as Genesis 1:1) for historical events, indicating that YHWH's acts in history are as categorically unique as the original creation.
11: 'Observe what I command you this day. Behold, I will drive out before you the Amorites, the Canaanites, the Hittites, the Perizzites, the Hivites, and the Jebusites': The covenant renewal re-states the territorial promise from Exodus 33:2. The six-nation expulsion list is the standard Deuteronomic formulation; its repetition here is deliberate — the covenant renewal does not subtract from the territorial promise despite the golden-calf rupture. YHWH's fidelity to the Abrahamic land-promise is unconditional; the Mosaic covenant's conditions govern how Israel inhabits the land, not whether they receive it.
12: 'Take care, lest you make a covenant with the inhabitants of the land to which you go, lest it become a snare in your midst': The prohibition on treaty-making with Canaan's inhabitants is the political application of exclusive covenant loyalty. Moqesh (snare/trap) — the same word used by Pharaoh's officials for Moses himself (Exodus 10:7). Covenants in the ANE created formal social bonds, including obligations of mutual aid and, often, cultural exchange including religious syncretism. The prohibition is not xenophobic but theological: the integrity of Israel's covenant with YHWH cannot coexist with formal treaty bonds to societies organized around rival deities.
13: 'You shall tear down their altars and break their pillars and cut down their Asherim': The three objects listed — mizbeach (altar), matsevah (standing stone/pillar), and asherah (carved wooden pole representing the Canaanite goddess Asherah) — are the three essential components of a Canaanite high-place. The destruction mandate is comprehensive: no coexistence with the physical infrastructure of alien worship. The archaeological record of Iron Age I Canaan shows exactly these types of cultic installations at numerous sites; the Deuteronomistic History's recurring critique is that Israel failed to destroy them (1 Kings 14:23; 2 Kings 23:14).
14: 'You shall worship no other god, for YHWH, whose name is Jealous (qanna), is a jealous God': The divine name Qanna (Jealous, H7067) appears only here and in Deuteronomy 4:24 and Exodus 6:15. Qin'ah (jealousy/zeal, H7065) in the divine context is not pettiness but the passionate, exclusive covenantal claim of a personal relationship: YHWH's jealousy is analogous to a husband's jealousy for exclusive relational fidelity, not arbitrary possessiveness. The OT's marriage metaphor for the covenant (Hosea, Jeremiah, Ezekiel) is anticipated in this vocabulary.
15: 'Lest you make a covenant with the inhabitants of the land, and when they whore after their gods and sacrifice to their gods and you are invited, you eat of his sacrifice': The path of apostasy runs through the social invitation to share in pagan sacrificial meals. The phrase wiznu acharei eloheihem (whore after their gods) introduces the sexual/covenant-infidelity metaphor that will dominate Hosea, Jeremiah, and Ezekiel. Participation in a sacrificial meal in the ancient world created a binding social and religious relationship — the same concern Paul addresses in 1 Corinthians 8-10 regarding food offered to idols in the Greco-Roman context.
16: 'And you take of their daughters for your sons, and their daughters whore after their gods and make your sons whore after their gods': The intermarriage prohibition is framed generationally: the first generation marries across the covenant boundary; the next generation inherits the religious syncretism. The social-transmission model of apostasy (through marriage, then children's formation) is precisely what the book of Judges dramatizes as the pattern of each generation's decline. Solomon's foreign wives and their altars (1 Kings 11:1-8) are the definitive royal case study of this exact pattern.
17: 'You shall not make for yourself any gods of cast metal (elohei massekhah)': The prohibition immediately after the social-contamination warning is no accident: the golden calf was an egel massekhah (cast metal calf, Exodus 32:4). The covenant renewal's specific re-statement of this prohibition in the context of intermarriage and syncretism completes the literary arc from the calf-making to the covenant-renewal: the law that was violated is the law that is reinscribed.
18: 'You shall keep the Feast of Unleavened Bread. Seven days you shall eat unleavened bread, as I commanded you, at the time appointed in the month Abib, for in the month Abib you came out from Egypt': The three pilgrimage feasts (Unleavened Bread/Passover, Weeks/Harvest, Booths/Ingathering) anchor the covenant renewal's ritual obligations. The repetition here from Exodus 23:14-17 in the so-called 'Book of the Covenant' suggests this is the ritual core of the renewed covenant, not merely a repetition. The agricultural calendar — Abib (spring barley), Weeks (summer wheat), Ingathering (autumn) — ties Israel's worship to the land's own rhythm, embedding covenant fidelity in the cycle of creation.
19: 'All that open the womb are mine, all your male livestock, the firstborn of cow and sheep': The firstborn dedication law restate from Exodus 13:2,12-15; its placement in the covenant renewal ties it directly to the Passover redemption — the firstborn of Israel was spared by YHWH's action; therefore the firstborn of every animal belongs to YHWH as acknowledgment of that redemption. The ritual economics of the tabernacle/temple system are grounded in this foundational ownership claim.
20: 'The firstborn of a donkey you shall redeem with a lamb, or if you will not redeem it you shall break its neck. All the firstborn of your sons you shall redeem. And none shall appear before me empty-handed': The donkey is the only clean-category animal that cannot be offered directly (being a work animal, not a sacrificial animal); it requires a substitute (lamb) or destruction. The firstborn son requires redemption — anticipating the later pidyon haben ceremony — grounding the obligation in the exodus rescue. Velo yera'u fanai reqam (none shall appear before me empty-handed) — the pilgrimage feast obligation to bring an offering is a covenantal requirement, not a voluntary gesture.
21: 'Six days you shall work, but on the seventh day you shall rest. In plowing time and in harvest you shall rest': The Sabbath re-statement within the covenant renewal adds a crucial agricultural precision: even during plowing and harvest — the two most time-critical agricultural periods — the Sabbath stands. The economic pressure of plowing season (seed must go in at the right moment) and harvest (grain must be cut before it falls) is the maximum test of Sabbath obedience. The covenant renewal specifically closes this exemption loophole.
22: 'You shall observe the Feast of Weeks (chag shavuot), the firstfruits of wheat harvest, and the Feast of Ingathering at the year's end': Shavuot (Weeks, H7620) — counted seven weeks from the Feast of Unleavened Bread; in Second Temple tradition it became associated with the giving of the Torah at Sinai, making it the annual commemoration of the covenant renewal event described in this very chapter. The Christian feast of Pentecost — fifty days after Passover — falls on the same calendar day and receives the outpouring of the Spirit, which is interpreted in Acts 2 as a new-covenant fulfillment of the Sinai gift of the law (Jeremiah 31:33).
23: 'Three times in the year shall all your males appear before YHWH God, the God of Israel': The three annual pilgrimage feasts create the structure of Israel's sacred year. Kol-zekhurcha (all your males) — the adult male population is obligated to appear; women, children, and slaves could attend but were not legally required. The centralization of these feasts at the later Temple (Deuteronomy 16:16) would make Jerusalem the pivot of Israelite national and religious identity for every Passover, Shavuot, and Sukkot generation.
24: 'For I will cast out nations before you and enlarge your borders; no one shall covet your land, when you go up to appear before YHWH your God three times in the year': The protection of Israel's land during the pilgrimage feasts — when the male population leaves their borders undefended — is an explicit divine promise. YHWH covenants to restrain the nations' appetite for Israel's territory during the three periods when Israel is most militarily vulnerable. The faith-structure is visible: going up to worship requires trusting God with the national security that worship's absence would seem to ensure.
25: 'You shall not offer the blood of my sacrifice with leaven, and the sacrifice of the Feast of the Passover shall not be left until the morning': The Passover regulations restated from Exodus 12:10,15 — no leaven with the blood, no overnight storage. The repetition in the covenant renewal context reinforces that these requirements are covenantally binding, not merely ceremonial best practices.
26: 'The best of the firstfruits of your ground you shall bring to the house of YHWH your God. You shall not boil a young goat in its mother's milk': The two regulations juxtaposed here — firstfruits offering and the milk-boiling prohibition — are the canonical pair. The firstfruits obligation (reshit bikkurei adamatekha) grounds Israel's agricultural relationship to the land in covenant gratitude. The prohibition on boiling a kid in its mother's milk appears three times (Exodus 23:19; Exodus 34:26; Deuteronomy 14:21) and became the founding text for the Jewish dietary separation of meat and dairy. Its original meaning is disputed: it may prohibit a specific Canaanite ritual (no direct evidence), or it may express a general ethic of not using a mother's life-giving substance to kill her offspring.
27: And YHWH said to Moses, 'Write these words, for in accordance with these words I have made a covenant with you and with Israel': The written character of the covenant is essential: it is not merely oral tradition but an inscribed, durable document. Moses writes the covenant terms; God writes the Ten Commandments on the tablets (v28). The dual-authorship structure of the written covenant — human scribe and divine inscriber — parallels the nature of the entire Torah as both humanly composed and divinely authorized.
28: So he was there with YHWH forty days and forty nights. He neither ate bread nor drank water. And he wrote on the tablets the words of the covenant, the Ten Commandments (aseret hadevarim): The second forty-day fast without food or water is, like the first (Exodus 24:18), a miraculous suspension of normal human limitation for the purpose of covenant mediation. Aseret ha-devarim (the Ten Words/Commandments) — the only place in Exodus where the Decalogue receives this summary name; Deuteronomy uses it as well (Exodus 4:13; Exodus 10:4). The phrase identifies the tablets' content as the foundational covenant text, distinct from the fuller legal corpus of Exodus 21-23 and Leviticus.
29: When Moses came down from Mount Sinai, with the two tablets of the testimony in his hand as he came down from the mountain, Moses did not know that the skin of his face shone (karan or panav) because he had been talking with God: Karan (H7160, to shoot out rays, to shine/radiate) is the Qal perfect of the verb derived from qeren (horn/ray). The homophony between shining and horning led Jerome in the Latin Vulgate (4th c. AD) to translate cornuta esset facies sua (his face was horned), which became the iconographic source for the famous horned Moses of Michelangelo's sculpture and medieval art generally. The correct meaning — radiance of light — is confirmed by the parallel in 2 Corinthians 3:7-18 where Paul uses the Greek doxa (glory) for Moses's shining face.
30: And when Aaron and all the people of Israel saw Moses, behold, the skin of his face shone, and they were afraid to come near him: The communal response of fear to Moses's radiance is theologically significant: the man who mediates YHWH's glory becomes himself a secondary source of holy terror. The same vocabulary of dread (yare'u) used for encountering YHWH in theophany (Exodus 3:6; Exodus 19:16-20) now applies to Moses's transformed face. Moses has become, through covenantal intimacy with God, a dangerous object requiring the same respectful distance as the divine presence itself.
31: But Moses called to them, and Aaron and all the leaders of the congregation returned to him, and Moses talked with them: Moses must actively call the people back; their fear is genuine. The leaders (nesi'ei ha-edah) return first — the appropriate hierarchy, moving from communal leadership to the broader assembly in v32. The pattern mirrors the Sinai theophany itself, where the people stood at a distance while Moses approached the thick darkness (Exodus 20:18-21).
32: And afterward all the people of Israel came near, and he commanded them all that YHWH had spoken with him on Mount Sinai: The full assembly receives Moses's instruction after the leaders. The covenant renewal is publicly communicated; it is not a private transaction between Moses and YHWH but the renewed basis of national life transmitted through the mediator to the entire community.
33: And when Moses had finished speaking with them, he put a veil (masveh) over his face: Masveh (H4533, veil/covering) — the veil is applied after speaking with the people, not during. Moses's face shines during divine encounter and human transmission; the veil covers the residual radiance when he is not actively conveying divine word. The social function of the veil is not to hide but to demarcate: the shining face is for covenant communication; ordinary time is veiled. Paul's interpretation in 2 Corinthians 3:13-18 is that Moses veiled his face to prevent Israel from seeing the 'end of what was passing away' — the fading glory — and uses this to argue that the new covenant's glory is permanent and unveiled.
34: Whenever Moses went in before YHWH to speak with him, he would remove the veil, until he came out. And when he came out and told the people of Israel what he was commanded': The veil-removal pattern: Moses removes the veil to enter YHWH's presence, receives instruction, exits, communicates it with the shining face exposed, then re-veils when the transmission is done. The rhythm maps the transmission cycle of revelation: divine source → mediator → community, with the shining face marking active transmission moments.
35: The people of Israel would see the face of Moses, that the skin of Moses's face was shining. And Moses would put the veil over his face again, until he went in to speak with him: The closing verse of the chapter creates the framework for interpreting all subsequent Mosaic teaching in Exodus and Leviticus: Moses's authority as teacher derives from his radiant, theophany-bearing face, which is the sign of his direct access to YHWH. The veil-cycle is the visual grammar of prophetic mediation in Israel's experience. Paul (2 Corinthians 3:18) contrasts this with believers in Christ who 'with unveiled face behold the glory of the Lord and are being transformed into the same image from one degree of glory to another.'
